metric_value: $520 million emerging Internet goods market; computer hardware and software comprised ~37%
notes: Aberdeen's market sizing for the early internet commerce opportunity.

metric_value: At least one computer hardware vendor at $1 million per day internet run rate
notes: Aberdeen data point illustrating rapid internet sales growth in 1997.

metric_name: Cost of in-house enterprise e-commerce development
metric_value: Greater than $1 million when requiring complex integration
notes: Aberdeen's benchmark for in-house complex e-commerce development costs.

metric_value: Internet consumers willing to wait 10-15 seconds for page download in 1997
notes: Aberdeen contrasts this with mainframe GUI research showing 2-3 second tolerance; predicts internet users' patience will erode quickly.

metric_value: Comprehensive enterprise internet retail solution with no technology strings attached; abstraction layer protects against single technology lock-in; supports CORBA, DCOM, multiple databases, EDI
notes: Aberdeen positions Commerce eXchange as the enterprise-grade, vendor-neutral alternative to point products.

metric_value: Production-class distributed transaction processing; remote object marshaling limits network communication; avoids messaging queue bottlenecks; CORBA/DCOM neutral via abstraction layer
notes: Aberdeen highlights Oasis's technical architecture as the key differentiator.

metric_value: Major commercial site deployable in weeks to months vs. in-house projects spanning a year or more
notes: Aberdeen documents the implementation speed advantage of Commerce eXchange.

metric_value: Fortune 1000 corporations use EDI for access to legacy data and common format for purchase orders, invoices, other business transactions
source_page: The Two Faces of Electronic Commerce
notes: Aberdeen confirms EDI's established role in Fortune 1000 supply chains as context for Commerce eXchange's EDI driver.

metric_name: CORBA vs DCOM enterprise internet standards battle
metric_value: Oracle, BroadVision promoting CORBA; Microsoft pushing DCOM; typical middleware translation gateways create performance hit; Oasis avoids this with neutral abstraction layer
notes: Aberdeen accurately characterizes the mid-1990s CORBA/DCOM standards battle.

metric_name: Primary obstacle for high-volume internet sites
metric_value: Lack of solution breadth; large-scale interactive sites must be open, scalable, and integrate multiple point products and legacy systems
notes: Aberdeen's central thesis framing the market need for integrated internet commerce platforms.

metric_value: Transaction engine; application portfolio; scalable open architecture; management/customization tools; single vendor relationship to limit supplier finger-pointing
notes: Aberdeen's four-part framework for evaluating internet commerce platforms.

metric_name: Web-enabled extensions of client-server apps inadequacy
metric_value: Web-enabled extensions of client-server or legacy applications will not adequately serve as Fortune 1000 Internet retail solutions; won't scale to millions of users; not designed for consumer interaction
notes: Aberdeen's argument for purpose-built internet commerce platforms over web-enabled legacy systems.

metric_value: Aberdeen strongly endorses Commerce eXchange; recommends it for corporations and entrepreneurs opening internet sales channels; positions it as having production-quality credentials with multiple successful deployments
notes: Aberdeen's central positive prediction about Commerce eXchange and InterWorld's market trajectory.

metric_name: 1997 as explosive year for internet commerce
metric_value: 1997 will see explosive internet sales for financial services, discount brokerages, industrial and consumer catalogs, travel services, food services, retail products from books to automobiles
notes: Aberdeen's market growth prediction for 1997 internet commerce.

metric_name: Object-oriented distributed transaction processing as the dominant internet commerce architecture
metric_value: Aberdeen believes InterWorld's technology vision reflects major trend toward OO programming and distributed transaction processing for networked businesses
notes: Aberdeen's architectural prediction about the future of internet commerce infrastructure.

metric_value: InterWorld Technology Ventures went public then pivoted to B2B; merged with DynamicWeb Enterprises (April 2000) creating eB2B Commerce, Inc.; eB2B filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy (October 2004); Commerce eXchange platform dissolved
notes: InterWorld failed despite Aberdeen's endorsement — a common outcome for 1990s e-commerce platform vendors.

metric_value: Internet commerce grew explosively through 1999-2000 as Aberdeen predicted; Amazon, eBay, and others achieved massive scale; however the dot-com crash (2000-2001) destroyed many first-generation internet retailers
source_page: Post-publication research: general internet commerce history
notes: Aberdeen's growth prediction was accurate for 1997-2000; the bust was not predicted.

metric_value: Distributed OO transaction processing for e-commerce proved directionally correct; modern e-commerce platforms (Shopify, Salesforce Commerce, Magento) use microservices/distributed architectures; however CORBA/DCOM specifically gave way to REST APIs and web services
source_page: Post-publication research: modern e-commerce platform architecture
notes: Aberdeen's architectural direction was correct; specific technologies (CORBA, DCOM) became obsolete.

metric_value: CORBA and DCOM both largely obsolete by 2005; replaced by XML/SOAP web services and later REST/JSON APIs; the abstraction layer approach InterWorld championed proved correct but manifested differently than 1997 technology battle suggested
source_page: Post-publication research: history of CORBA and DCOM
notes: CORBA/DCOM battle that Aberdeen analyzed was a historical footnote by 2005.

metric_value: Digital software delivery (InterWorld's Digital Delivery product concept) proved highly prescient; Apple App Store (2008), Steam (2003), and digital distribution became the dominant software distribution model; InterWorld's technology did not survive but the market category exploded
source_page: Post-publication research: digital distribution market history
notes: One of Aberdeen's most prescient observations — digital goods delivery became massive by 2010.

metric_value: Aberdeen expects internet consumers' patience for slow sites to peter out quickly; 10-15 seconds accepted in 1997 but declining rapidly
notes: Aberdeen predicted the shift in user experience expectations that drove performance engineering in e-commerce.

metric_value: Technology-savvy Broderbund uses InterWorld Digital Delivery for electronic software distribution
notes: Broderbund cited as reference customer validating digital goods delivery use case.

metric_value: Sells 20,000+ computer products and distributes Microsoft software titles using Commerce eXchange
notes: MicroWarehouse cited as a large-scale computer retail reference customer.
